did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a dual-architecture ensemble deep learning model (Mel-XNet) combining ResNet-50 and DenseNet-121 CNNs for melanoma classification on dermoscopic images. It also employs Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations (LIME) for explainability.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents Mel-XNet, a novel dual-architecture ensemble deep learning model for melanoma detection. It details original experimental work including methodology (ResNet-50 and DenseNet-121 integration, LIME explainability), dataset usage (ISIC 2018), training procedures (5-fold cross-validation), and quantitative results (95.25% accuracy, 0.9904 AUC). Although published in a conference proceedings (ICCIT 2025), it reports original empirical results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Mel-XNet: A Dual-Architecture Ensemble for High-Accuracy Melanoma Detection with Integrated Explainability
journal: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
brief_summary: This study proposes Mel-XNet, an explainable dual-architecture ensemble deep learning model for high-accuracy melanoma detection using dermoscopic images. The framework integrates ResNet-50 and DenseNet-121 CNNs trained via 5-fold cross-validation on the ISIC 2018 dataset, which contains 13,879 images of benign and malignant lesions. A soft voting ensemble of ten resulting models was evaluated on a hold-out test set, achieving an accuracy of 95.25% and an AUC of 0.9904. The model incorporates Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations (LIME) to provide visual justifications for its predictions, addressing the black-box nature of deep learning. Results demonstrate that Mel-XNet outperforms its constituent base models and competes favorably with state-of-the-art methods while offering clinical interpretability.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target of the binary classification task. The dataset contains 6,590 malignant images out of 13,879 total images.

The negative class in the binary classification task. The dataset contains 7,289 benign images. The paper does not specify individual benign subtypes (e.g., nevus, seborrheic keratosis) but groups them as 'benign'.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of dermoscopic images into benign or malignant melanoma categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'melanoma classification' and 'benign versus malignant' binary classification. The model outputs probability vectors for two classes, and the final prediction is determined by soft voting to select the class with the highest mean probability. Metrics reported include accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC for this binary diagnostic task.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018 (International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2018)
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset of dermoscopic images of skin lesions, also referred to in the text as the 'publicly accessible Melanoma Cancer Image Dataset'.

use.training: Approximately 85% of the data (11,879 images) was allocated for the cross-validation training process.
use.validation: Used within the 5-fold cross-validation strategy on the training subset.
use.testing: The remaining 15% (2,000 images) was reserved as an independent hold-out test set to evaluate generalization performance.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To train and evaluate the base models (ResNet-50 and DenseNet-121) and mitigate overfitting.
To combine predictions from ten resulting models (from the 5-fold CV of two architectures) to derive the final classification.
To evaluate the generalization performance of the Mel-XNet ensemble on independent data (2,000 images).
To report True Positives (948) and True Negatives (957) and assess classification performance.
To demonstrate the model's discriminative ability, reporting an AUC of 0.9904.
To mitigate class imbalance during training by reducing the contribution of well-classified samples.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made based on point estimates of accuracy and AUC without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2018.notes: Mel-XNet outperformed its constituent base models (ResNet-50 and DenseNet-121). The ensemble achieved an accuracy of 95.25% and an AUC of 0.9904. Confusion matrix analysis showed 948 true positives and 957 true negatives. The model was compared against state-of-the-art methods including SCCNet (95.20%), YOLOv8x (86.20%), Custom Xception (91.09%), and CNN Ensemble (94.50%).

ResNet-50_5_Fold_CV.notes: Training dynamics showed stable learning and convergence with insignificant gaps between training and validation accuracies, indicating effective handling of overfitting.

DenseNet-121_5_Fold_CV.notes: Training dynamics showed consistent and robust training performance. DenseNet-121 achieved a slightly higher average accuracy (93.14%) compared to ResNet-50 (92.62%) during cross-validation.

architecture_used.main_model: Mel-XNet (Dual-Architecture Ensemble of ResNet-50 and DenseNet-121)

architecture_details.ensemble_method: Soft voting ensemble of 10 models (5 folds for each of the 2 architectures)
architecture_details.resnet_features: Residual connections to mitigate vanishing gradient problem
architecture_details.densenet_features: Dense connectivity for feature reuse and propagation
architecture_details.classifier_head: New fully connected layer for binary classification (benign vs malignant), replacing original FC layer

training_details.early_stopping: Stopped if no progress in last 5 consecutive epochs

classification_or_output_head.description: Averages probability vectors from 10 base models; class with highest mean probability is selected

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing input images to a uniform dimension of 224 × 224 pixels
Normalization of pixel values using the mean and standard deviation from the ImageNet dataset

Data augmentation including random resized cropping (80-100% area), horizontal flipping (50% probability), random rotation (±15 degrees), and color jittering (brightness/contrast ±0.2, saturation ±0.1, hue ±0.05)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Selecting a crop covering 80% to 100% of the original image area and resizing it.
Applied within a range of ±15 degrees.
Randomly adjusted brightness and contrast by up to 0.2, saturation by up to 0.1, and hue by up to 0.05.

conclusions: The proposed Mel-XNet framework, a dual-architecture ensemble of ResNet-50 and DenseNet-121, achieved an accuracy of 95.25% and an AUC of 0.9904 on the ISIC 2018 hold-out test set, significantly outperforming its constituent base models.
Mel-XNet demonstrated superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods, including SCCNet (95.20%), CNN Ensemble (94.50%), Custom Xception (91.09%), and YOLOv8x (86.20%).

The integration of Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations (LIME) provided visual justifications for predictions, highlighting clinically significant features such as irregular borders and pigmentation for malignant cases and uniform features for benign cases, thereby enhancing model transparency and trust.
The use of a soft voting ensemble of ten models derived from 5-fold cross-validation resulted in a more rigorous and less sensitive final prediction mechanism compared to single-model approaches.

The study acknowledges limitations regarding generalizability, noting that dermatology datasets often suffer from class imbalance and visual similarity between benign and malignant lesions, and suggests future work should focus on larger, multi-institutional datasets and transformer-based architectures.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC 2018 dataset, which is a single, publicly available source; the authors acknowledge that generalizability to broader clinical settings requires validation on larger, multi-institutional datasets.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of performance improvements over state-of-the-art methods; comparisons are based on point estimates.
The dataset exhibits class imbalance (7,289 benign vs. 6,590 malignant images), which the authors note can lead to biased models, though they mitigated this using Focal Loss.
The model was evaluated on a hold-out test set of 2,000 images, but there is no evidence of external validation on independent datasets from different institutions or demographics.
The paper acknowledges the inherent visual similarity between benign nevi and early melanomas as a limitation that affects differentiation accuracy.
The implementation was conducted on a single NVIDIA P100 GPU, which may limit reproducibility for researchers without similar high-end hardware resources.

The comparison with state-of-the-art methods (e.g., SCCNet, YOLOv8x) uses reported accuracy figures from other papers rather than re-running those models on the same test set, which may introduce comparison bias due to differing preprocessing or evaluation protocols.
